videoTitle: Visa Pathways â€” What's Actually Realistic
landscapeTheme: Theme Park â€” teamwork, talent, massive operations engine

scenes.heading: Earning Your Backstage Pass
scenes.narration: In the last lesson, you learned who belongs on your team and how the U.S. classifies each role. Now the question turns to you: what pass gets you through the gate? In Central Florida's entertainment district, nobody walks backstage without the right credential. The same is true for working in the U.S. Owning a company does not by itself give you the right to work here. A Colombian founder with fifty thousand fintech users assumed she could fly to Miami and run a U.S. office. Her tourist visa did not allow it. Important: this content is educational only. All visa and immigration actions should be reviewed with a qualified immigration attorney.

scenes.heading: O-1A â€” The Headliner Pass
bullets: For founders with extraordinary ability or sustained recognition
Evidence: published articles, revenue growth, speaking engagements, awards, patents
Need at least three of eight USCIS criteria
Cost: five to ten thousand dollars plus fees â€” timeline: three to six months
scenes.narration: In a theme park, headliners get top billing because their talent draws the crowd. The O-1A works the same way. It is for founders whose track record speaks for itself. You need evidence of sustained national or international recognition: published articles, significant revenue growth, speaking engagements, awards, or patents. You must meet at least three of eight USCIS criteria. Cost runs five to ten thousand dollars in attorney fees plus government filing fees. Standard processing takes three to six months. Premium processing is available at an additional fee for fifteen business days. If your achievements already put you on the marquee, this credential opens the widest door.

scenes.heading: E-2 â€” The Park Investor Pass
bullets: For founders investing substantial capital in a U.S. business â€” typically one hundred thousand plus
Your country must have a treaty with the U.S. â€” many LATAM and European countries qualify
Does not lead directly to a green card
Cost: three to eight thousand dollars plus fees â€” timeline: four to eight months
scenes.narration: Every major attraction needs capital behind it. The E-2 Treaty Investor visa is for founders who are investing real money into a U.S. operation, typically one hundred thousand dollars or more. Think of it as buying a stake in the park itself. You are funding the ride, not just riding it. Your country must have a treaty with the United States. Many Latin American and European countries qualify. Important detail: the E-2 does not lead directly to a green card. Cost runs three to eight thousand dollars in attorney fees plus the investment capital itself. Timeline is four to eight months through consular processing, and it varies by country.

scenes.heading: L-1A and H-1B â€” Transfer and Lottery
bullets: L-1A: for founders transferring from an existing foreign company â€” need one year in a managerial role
H-1B: employer-sponsored, annual lottery with roughly 25% selection rate
L-1A cost: four to ten thousand â€” H-1B cost: two to five thousand
H-1B lottery in March, start date in October â€” timing is rigid
scenes.narration: Theme park chains move experienced managers between locations all the time. The L-1A works the same way. It is for founders who already run a company outside the U.S. and want to open or manage the American operation. You need a managerial or executive role at the foreign entity for at least one year within the last three years. Cost runs four to ten thousand dollars. Then there is the H-1B, which is essentially a lottery ticket. Employer-sponsored, with an annual lottery that selects roughly twenty-five percent of applicants. Less common for founders. The timing is rigid: lottery in March, start date in October. If your situation does not clearly fit one of these four pathways, an immigration attorney can help identify alternative options.

scenes.heading: Choosing Your Guide â€” The Right Immigration Attorney
bullets: Look for an attorney who specializes in business immigration, not general practice
Referrals from other founders in the UCF BIP network are often the most reliable
Be cautious of anyone who guarantees outcomes
Prepare: passport, visa docs, business operations proof, achievements evidence, U.S. business plan
scenes.narration: In any complex park, you want a guide who knows which doors lead where. Look for an attorney who specializes in business immigration, not general practice. Central Florida has a strong base of immigration attorneys experienced with international entrepreneurs. Referrals from other founders in the UCF BIP network are often more reliable than directory listings. Be cautious of anyone who guarantees outcomes â€” nobody can promise which credential you will receive. Prepare for your consultation with your passport, existing visa documentation, proof of business operations, evidence of achievements, and your U.S. business plan. Start exploring visa options six to twelve months before you need to be working in the U.S. Rushing leads to weak applications and denials.

scenes.heading: A Founder Finds Her Path
scenes.narration: Let me give you an example of how this works in practice. A founder from Mexico owned a profitable e-commerce company and had one hundred twenty thousand dollars to invest in a U.S. operation. She had no extraordinary achievements or awards and had never managed a U.S.-affiliated company. Her immigration attorney explored all four pathways. The O-1A did not fit because she lacked the extraordinary ability evidence. The L-1A did not fit because there was no existing qualifying relationship between a foreign and U.S. company. The H-1B was lottery-based and timing did not work. The E-2 Treaty Investor visa fit perfectly. Mexico has a treaty with the U.S. and she had substantial capital to invest. She was working in her Orlando office within six months.

scenes.heading: Start the Process Early
scenes.narration: Four backstage passes, four different doors â€” but none are handed out automatically. Start the visa process six to twelve months before you need to be working in the U.S. Work with an immigration attorney who specializes in business immigration and who will be honest about which credential fits your situation. Evaluate which pathway matches your track record and capital, then create a realistic schedule. The right legal guidance early on prevents expensive mistakes later. Next stop: the operations center of the theme park â€” payroll, taxes, and the real cost of keeping a team running.
